title: Chapter 8: Where the Horse Dies
content: <!-- Speed: Breather | D12+D1 -->
Shadowfax died at a Shell station in Santa Monica.

Not dramatically. Not in flames. Just a grinding noise, a shudder, and then silence — the kind of silence that mechanics call "catastrophic" and everyone else calls "the sound of money leaving your bank account."
Sam turned the key. Nothing. Turned it again. Still nothing. The dashboard lights flickered like a dying firefly.
"The Royal Steed has fallen," Chloe said quietly.
"The Royal Steed needs a new transmission. Which costs more than the car is worth."
They sat in the silence. The Shell station's fluorescent lights hummed. A man at pump 4 was eating a gas station burrito with the expression of someone who had given up on joy.
"What do we do?" Sam asked.
Chloe didn't answer immediately. Which was unusual. Chloe always had an answer.
"I don't know," Chloe said.
Sam turned to look at her. The crown was still on. The poncho was still draped. But something in Chloe's face had shifted. A crack. A tiny one.
"You always know."
"I always pretend to know. There's a difference."
Sam didn't say anything for a long moment. The burrito man at pump 4 finished his meal and drove away. The Shell station was quiet.
"Chloe," Sam said finally. "What are we actually doing?"
"I don't know."
"No. I mean — you. What are you actually doing?"
Chloe stared at the dashboard. The dead dashboard. "I had a breakdown in a Zoom meeting and I haven't stopped having it for five days."
"I know."
"And everyone thinks I'm crazy."
"I know."
"And maybe I am crazy."
Sam was quiet. Then: "Maybe."
Chloe flinched. Just slightly.

"But here's the thing," Sam continued. "You're crazy in a way that makes sense. You called that CEO a dark lord and he was a dark lord. You called those influencers vampires and they were vampires. You called Kevin a grifter and he was a grifter. You're not seeing things that aren't there. You're seeing things and giving them weirder names."
Chloe looked at her. The crack in her expression widened, then closed, then did something else entirely.
"You think so?"
"I've been keeping a spreadsheet for five days. Every person you've 'diagnosed' has turned out to be exactly what you said they were. The only thing you're wrong about is the terminology."
Chloe processed this. "So I'm not crazy. I'm just... poetically accurate."
"You're clinically observant with a fantasy vocabulary."
"That's the nicest thing anyone has ever said to me."

They abandoned the car — not permanently, just for the night — and walked to the beach. Santa Monica Pier glowed in the distance. The Pacific was dark and endless and didn't care about follower counts or quarterly reports or whether you'd had a psychotic break in a Zoom meeting.
They sat on the sand. Shoes off. Crown still on.
"This is Avalon," Chloe said.
"This is Santa Monica."
"Same thing. Look at it. Look at the water."

Sam looked. The water was doing what water does: arriving, retreating, arriving again. It had been doing this for billions of years. It would be doing this long after every influencer and every CEO and every wellness guru had been forgotten.
"Okay," Sam said. "Maybe it's Avalon."
Chloe turned to her. "Really?"
"No. But I'm willing to pretend for tonight."
Chloe smiled. The crack had closed. Or maybe it hadn't been a crack at all — maybe it had been a window opening.
They slept on the beach. Not intentionally — they just didn't leave. The sand was cold but the air was warm and the sound of the waves was better than any white noise machine Sam had ever paid for.

In the morning, a jogger found them: two women, one in a plastic crown, one clutching a phone with a dead battery, both covered in sand, both somehow looking more at peace than anyone the jogger had seen all year.
"Are you... okay?" the jogger asked.
Chloe opened one eye. "We're in Avalon."
"You're on the beach."
"Same thing."
The jogger jogged away. She would tell people about the two women on the beach for weeks. No one would believe her.
A tow truck retrieved Shadowfax at 10 AM. The driver, a man named Gus who had been towing cars in LA for thirty years, took one look at Chloe's crown and said: "You're that lady from TikTok."
"Queen Artemis of Avalon."
"My wife loves you. Said you did more for her mental health in three minutes than her therapist did in two years."
Chloe blinked. "Your wife?"
"Yeah. She watched the strawberry farm video, called her mom for the first time in eight months, and cried for like an hour. Good cry, she said. The kind you need."
Sam and Chloe exchanged a look.
"Where are you headed?" Gus asked.
"We're already here," Chloe said. "This is Avalon."
Gus looked at Santa Monica — the pier, the tourists, the homeless encampment, the guy selling CBD dog treats. "This?"
"Every kingdom looks ordinary from the outside," Chloe said. "That's the trick. Avalon has always been here. You just have to name it."
